data.title: Alien
data.notes: cifo - verified - alternate is 9 blocks different

data.title: Ballblazer (NTSC)
data.notes: cifo - verified - alternate version of this original that differs by 61sectors is also verified

data.title: Blue Max
data.notes: cifo - there are missing/corrupted sectors that read differently on every copy.  for this reason there are alternates kept, but the game is identical.  The game is written on both sides of the disk, and they are a different sector layout - I don't know if the game plays different

data.title: Conan
data.protection: custom format, long sectors won't fit in drive ram normally during serial nibble.  this format has 3 long sectors per custom track.

data.title: Fort Apocalypse
data.protection: checks e2 on t22s14, lots of other unchecked errors
data.notes: cifo - all disks have 11 sectors that have bad GCR, so they will never match between disks or even reads of the same disk 100%.

data.title: Gamemaker
data.notes: cifo - verified - I was missing sides 3 and 4 which contain an example game and some more graphics, but added now from Daryl.

data.title: Hot Wheels
data.notes: cifo - 2 alternate images differ a lot on several tracks, but in the same sectors, so it could be a lot of save game data.  disk ships notched.

data.title: Indiana Jones in the Lost Kingdom (V2)
data.protection: checks signature on t12s20. e5 on t1s1 unchecked?
data.notes: cifo - verified - I am assuming this is the newer release due to the disk format id being "V2".  The other release doesn't have this...

data.title: Infiltrator
data.notes: cifo - verified - cannot reduce syncs when remastered or protection will fail.  alternate version seems older and protection fails on VICE in NTSC (but works in PAL).  This is probably a bug fix to the protection in the other release since it fails in the same way as the protection check.

data.title: Beyond the Black Hole
data.protection:
After the title screen, it reads track 16 sector 20 (which seems to be mostly empty), after the read the drive head is at the tail gap (normally $55 bytes). It reads until it gets a $6A byte, puts it in the $309 location in drive memory. Then it reads the next 4 bytes and puts them reversed at $305-$308. Standard GCR decode follows and data is sent to the C64.

The C64 loads the data into $CB00, then EORs the 4 bytes it loaded together. Result must be $79 or reset occurs.

Force the value for a crack (I didn't check for checksums or anything like that) and the program continues and asks you to insert side 2, which I don't have. Or fix the GCR bytes in the image so they EOR (after GCR decode) to $79, which shouldn't be too hard.

BTW, the non-working bytes in the NIB dump you have are at offset $3DE97 and are:

6A 3D 5C D1 9F

This string of bytes appears to be written at a different density level, that's why it's coming in incorrectly.  Interesting.

(LORD CRASS)

data.title: Flak
data.protection:
syncr0l0k - This awful little protection writes to the disk. It writes to t/s 19/02 and that must succeed or it tries over and over. Then it tries writing to t/s 20/02. This must fail or it tries over and over again.

The protection seems to be based on physically damaged sectors that need to return errors when attempts are made to write to them. I tried erasing the sector header for 20/02 to see if it would fool the protection, but it doesn't work. The protection checks that some of the sector was written (first 6 bytes at the minimum must match).  After it writes to track 20, it reads it back to verify it. The data it writes is a pseudo-random byte taken from the timer, so it's different each time you load the game.

When it reads it back, at least 6 bytes must match what it thinks it wrote, but the whole sector cannot match. If it does: protection failed. 

Unless someone can think of a way to damage the sector in an image to the extent that a U2 write command will only partially work, this won't function in an emulator.

And unless you can physically damage a real disk at the right spot, this won't work remastered either.

On the other hand, it's an easy crack to remove the protection check entirely. There's only one little method used to hide it (check $179F in the main program for the self-modifying code that hides the protection check at $17A2).  

(LordCrass)

data.title: Kwik-Write!
data.protection:
Track 15 is non-standard. Only the last sector has a sector header and any data in the data block. This data block is divided into 3 shorter sections by sync marks:

The first section has $45 bytes of data and is written at a non-standard density (%10 instead of %11).

The second section is written at density %01 and $7D bytes are read in.

The third section is written at density %00 and $82 more bytes are read in. The GCR data is then decoded using standard ROM GCR decoding routines.

This track needs to be redumped three more times, at density levels 0, 1, and 2. The data could then be merged into the image for a working G64.

In short, there's all 4 density levels present on this one track (the sector header is written at standard density %11)

data.title: Microleague Wresting (WWF)
data.protection:
checks signature on t35 before match, goes back to main menu if failed and does announcements only.

The "signature" on t35 is a small byte variation ($4A 55 73 55) in the tail gap of Sector 0.  If you replace the "$73" with "$55", the protection will fail, simple but effective, and very easy to miss if you don't know what you are looking for.
BTW: the same string (4A 55 73 55) is found on Track 15 Sector 9.  I can only guess the reason it was put there, some kind of subtle hint? maybe the idea was to have the crackers work like mad, chasing their tails, only to realize at the last possible moment that the answer was hiding in plain sight all along?
My thinking behind this is that throughout the loading process, track 15 isn't even touched, so it can't be checking for a string match, perhaps the string on t15 is scanned for at a later stage, or when performing certain actions? (Stephen)

data.title: Cop Shocker (PAL)
data.protection:
Checks non-standard density within a sector.

1. Seeks to track 35, sector 1
2. Reads 41 GCR bytes after the sync mark, they must all be $5A.
3. Changes to highest density.
4. Read 3 GCR bytes, remembering the 3rd byte.
5. The next 10 GCR bytes must all be the same as that 3rd byte.

The image is easily fixed for emulators by making those 11 bytes all the same (ie. $65). Fixing this for writing back to real diskette requires a special writing routine that can write this sector with the required non-standard density.

data.notes: cifo - game seems the same as "Fist" USA release. Could just be a name change.
